text:
Request for Moderator Review — Permanent Account Suspension Linked to Secondary Account

Hello Community Team, I am respectfully requesting a moderator review regarding the permanent suspension placed on my long-standing eBay account ,thnk_dffrnt007, which I believe may warrant reconsideration based on my account history and the circumstances surrounding the action. I have been an active member of the eBay marketplace for over seven years and have consistently maintained a strong seller performance record. Throughout this time, I upheld Top Rated Seller status, maintained near-perfect feedback, shipped orders promptly, and ensured high standards of customer service. My account history reflects reliability, professionalism, and full commitment to marketplace policies. Recently, my primary account was permanently suspended after being linked to a secondary account registered under the same residential address. While I understand the importance of policy enforcement and account integrity, my primary account has always remained compliant and was not directly involved in policy violations. The suspension appears to have resulted from account association rather than the individual performance and conduct of my main seller account. This account is very important to my business operations, as I rely on eBay to buy and sell electronics and have built my small business on the platform over many years. I am fully committed to complying with all marketplace policies and maintaining the high standards reflected in my long-term performance history. I would sincerely appreciate it if a moderator could review my case and advise whether my primary account may be eligible for reconsideration based on its individual record and positive standing within the marketplace community. Thank you very much for your time, support, and guidance.
title: Request for Moderator Review — Permanent Account Suspension Linked to Secondary Account

text:
Want to increase sales for my clothing items

I used to be a shopaholic, and over the years I have bought A LOT of items and now have storage units. I have noticed that my sales have slowed in the last two years (especially international sales) and I want to sell my items much quicker than I am currently selling them. In the past I used to sell at least 1-3 items a day, but now I’m struggling to sell one item a week. I have tried promoted listings, vouchers, free postage, sales etc and these have had a minimal impact. I am aware the landscape of selling on eBay has changed since the removal of selling fees for private sellers and also the US tariffs (50% of my custom was from USA). I’m also aware that my items are high value - and the clothing category is extremely saturated. However, I don’t want to sell too low. My items are either gently Preloved or new and my goal is to just sell all my items as I’m not acquiring anymore. I would really appreciate advice (or a coach) on what I can be doing to increase my sales. I have thousands of items to sell and I’d like to get down to one storage unit by the end of the year. What do people recommend? My dream would be to sell at least 70-100 items a week. I know it can be possible: I just need to know what I need to do and how to do it! Thanks in advance.
title: Want to increase sales for my clothing items

text:
Account Below Standard

On November 20th, I received a notice from ebay that my account is now considered below standard. The numbers were an average from a 12 month period which put my defect rate around 2.7%. Almost all of the defects were having to cancel an item because I couldn't find it. Looking back, I should have put my store in vacation mode a few times while I worked on organizing my inventory and the 3 platforms they are on. I work a full-time job in addition to selling on ebay, etc. but I'll be retiring in June and will have a lot more time to get things in order. In the meantime, I've tried everything I can think of to get out of below-standard status. I've had a 40% off clearance sale, offered a discount coupon every month, improved listings, photos, and most importantly, I haven't had any more defects since November. Unfortunately, though, I'm still below standard, and I'm not sure how long it will take to improve. I'm not allowed to promote my items as a below-standard seller, so my items are getting no boost in visibility at all. My sales have plummeted since November, so it's harder to improve my defect percentage rate. It's safe to say I've learned my lesson in a major way and want to get back on track with ebay but what I've done so far hasn't worked. Any ideas?
title: Account Below Standard

text:
Re: I don’t want to use authenticity guarantee. How do I remove it from my listing?.

I have 2 comments that I'll make separately: animiss wrote: I know it’s authentic. I don’t know the authenticators. Has to get mailed twice. Takes longer to get to the buyer. I am anxious until it gets delivered to buyer. Most buyers know and understand how the AG (authenticity guaranteed) program works and they appreciate the extra comfort in knowing that their purchases will be inspected. They often purposely buy items that will go to the authenticator. They know there are 2 steps to the process and that items are not shipped directly to them. animiss wrote: I tried international shipping through eBay and they lost the item. I finally got my money back, buyer never got item and was mad at me. Neutral rating. Just don’t trust it. If you use EIS (ebay international shipping) and an item is lost after tracking shows delivery to Glendale, IL, you are protected and you don't have to fight for your money. Ebay covers losses in those cases. And if you received a neutral feedback for non-receipt on an EIS item for which tracking shows delivery, that's removable. You are cutting off your nose to spite your face.
title: Re: I don’t want to use authenticity guarantee. How do I remove it from my listing?.

Have you filed a Return Request? Within 30 days from date of delivery, go to your Purchase History, find the item, and under “More actions” click on “Return item” and select the reason for the return. Attach a photo if required. IF your request is approved, the seller will have 3 business days to either accept the return and provide a prepaid return shipping label OR refund without having you return the item. If they don't do either, you can go back to your Return Request details 4 business days after you filed the Request and ask Ebay to step in.

Next day my accout had this initiated . I got no warning and this is not a recent issue . It happened because I had no heat for months in freezing weather and my landlord would not fix it so we had to but I can’t ship products when I’ve got fuel oil and dirt on my hands and sell collectrable cards. The customer service person wrote the grievance but kept telling me that it’s based on a year so I’m sure it wasn’t written to help me . text:
Is it reasonable that a timing policy overrides counterfeit authentication evidence?

Hello everyone, I’d like to ask for opinions regarding eBay’s 3-day trading card policy , because I recently had a situation where it felt very difficult as a buyer. Here is what happened. I won an auction for a high-value trading card after bidding for some time. At the time of delivery, I was temporarily overseas , so before shipment I sent the seller a message asking if they could ship a few days later and preferably use signature confirmation , since the card had significant value. The seller confirmed the message. However, the item was still shipped immediately and delivered while I was away. The package was left at my front door without signature. Naturally I was very worried about the package sitting outside for few days. When I returned home and opened the package, I realized the card appeared to be counterfeit . I then opened a case with eBay, provided screenshots of my conversation with the seller, showed proof that I was overseas, submitted third-party authentication documentation indicating the card was not authentic. Despite all of this, the case was ultimately denied because the request was outside the 3-day timeframe for trading cards . This is where I feel confused. Even though the item was determined by a third-party to be counterfeit, the case was still denied because of the 3-day rule. So my question for the community is: Is it reasonable that a timing policy overrides counterfeit authentication evidence? I fully understand the need for strict timelines, but in situations involving counterfeit items , it seems that authenticity verification should carry significant weight. Has anyone else encountered similar situations with high-value trading cards? I would appreciate hearing other collectors’ perspectives or suggestions for how buyers can better protect themselves in cases like this. Thank you.
title: Is it reasonable that a timing policy overrides counterfeit authentication evidence?

text:
Concern about counterfeit case being redirected to delivery damage claim

Hello everyone, I recently experienced something confusing during a dispute involving a counterfeit trading card, and I wanted to ask if anyone in the community has encountered something similar. I purchased a trading card that later turned out to be counterfeit. I provided eBay with detailed photos, comparison analysis, and third-party authentication documentation confirming that the card is not authentic . Based on this evidence, I requested a refund from eBay for receiving a counterfeit item. However, instead of the case focusing on the authenticity issue, the situation seemed to shift in another direction. The seller later attempted to frame the issue as a “shipping damage” situation , even though my dispute was strictly about the item being counterfeit. I received an email through eBay that appeared to be sent from the seller , asking me to file a “delivery damage claim.” It also made me concerned that if I clicked and submitted that claim, the case could potentially be redirected into a delivery damage insurance claim , rather than addressing the counterfeit issue itself. From a buyer’s perspective, it felt like this could allow the situation to be reframed as shipping damage , where the seller might receive compensation from the shipping insurance, while the counterfeit issue would no longer be the focus of the case and as the buyer might still not receive a refund. Has anyone in the community seen situations where a counterfeit dispute ends up being redirected into a delivery damage claim? Any insight would be appreciated.
title: Concern about counterfeit case being redirected to delivery damage claim

FeedBacks  for  Buyers

I am constantly getting messages form eBay advising me this= Leaving feedback is an important part of buying and selling on eBay. I wonder if eBay notify the Buyer to leave the Seller feedback? I am constantly receiving automated messages from eBay stating that 'leaving feedback is an important part of buying and selling.' However, I find this one-sided and unfair. I provide exceptional service for every order: I offer significant price reductions, provide gift wrapping, include handwritten thank-you cards, and ensure same-day shipping. Despite these efforts, many of these buyers never leave feedback. Even when I follow up politely to confirm they received their order, my messages are ignored. It feels 'mean-spirited' for eBay to constantly pressure me to leave feedback for buyers who do not reciprocate, especially when eBay’s policy prevents sellers from leaving anything but positive feedback. This system protects 'problem' buyers while offering no recourse for sellers who go above and beyond. Because of this, I have decided to block any buyers who ignore my communication after receiving these extra benefits. They do not deserve my feedback; they deserve to be blocked from my shop.
title: FeedBacks  for  Buyers

I would like to share my experience with a recent purchase in order to warn other collectors. [Removed by moderator] The card I received was later inspected by KSA Certification (a professional authentication service) and was officially confirmed as counterfeit. The inspection report states that the card is a reproduction with pixelized printing, incorrect color separation, and inconsistent card stock compared with the authentic Bandai version. Collectors purchasing high-value vintage Bandai Yu-Gi-Oh cards should be cautious and consider independent authentication when buying similar items. I hope this information helps other buyers avoid purchasing counterfeit collectibles.

text:
Tax Season Help - SSN Change?

Anyone out there with some helpful advice or guidance would be most appreciated as I am at complete a loss right now on what my next move should be. I've read these forums on multiple occasions over the years and have gotten some pretty helpful advice but have never posted a question myself, so just want to thank anyone who is active out there for reaching out to help the eBay community as it is most appreciated. Here is my dilemma - I have been selling on eBay for about 5 years now and over the last year my sales have really taken off way more than I could have ever imagined (around 20k in sales annually). When I first set up the account it was under my mothers name and SSN as I was helping my folks sell a lot of their own items that had value (lifelong garage sale hoarders, mostly vintage collectibles) and the money would go directly to her bank account so she could declare it all on her own taxes. Over the years I have begun collecting and selling a lot of my own items and have pretty much taken the store over as my own after selling off the majority of my parents stuff for them. Would like to have the SSN switched over to my own SSN so the yearly 1099k form can be sent to me and give them a break on the taxes from now on as a lot of what i'm selling nowadays is for my own personal profit. A year ago I switched our bank accounts over so that the money would go directly to my account with my SSN and have been reimbursing them out of pocket for whatever I sold of theirs since the switch. Didn't realize the 1099k was sent to the eBay account SSN number, thought it would be sent to the SSN number with the bank account that was receiving the payments. So yeah all of this money is being taxed to her when I would prefer to be the one receiving the 1099k form so they don't have the tax burden to deal with from this point forward. Is there any way to change the account over to my SSN or does this mean what I fear - that I have to start a completely new account from scratch with selling limits and zero feedback? Selling on eBay has been something I've come to enjoy and have always had good feedback and customer service always try to ship carefully within 24-48 hours and enjoy talking to people who reach out about items with questions or concerns. Really hate to give up this store account that I've built as I have put a lot of time and effort into it over the years. Have 100% positive feedback with over 1800 sales and over 320 listings active right now. Can I set up a new account with my personal info, SSN, and bank account and link it to this one to at least transfer the listings? The account I'm posting from is my own personal account and would like to link I've never sold anything using it but have made some purchases.
title: Tax Season Help - SSN Change?

text:
For those who wonder about slow sales.....

This is what sellers are dealing with in today's eBay market (not my words)....... 1. Algorithm Changes Favor "Newness" Over Reputation Stale Listing Penalties: Listings that remain unsold for more than 14 days without engagement are flagged as "low-velocity," leading to a "decay loop" where they receive fewer and fewer views. Simply having a "good" item is not enough; it must be "fresh." Mobile-First Filtering: Over 74% of purchases occur on mobile, and eBay's mobile app applies strict filters for image speed, information density, and "trust badges" (like Fast & Free). A perfectly good desktop listing might be suppressed on mobile if it lacks these optimizations. Semantic Search vs. Keywords: The search engine now uses semantic indexing, which means it looks for context rather than just matching keywords. If titles do not follow the specific Brand + Model + Key Modifier structure, they may become invisible. 2. The "Pay-to-Play" Environment (Promoted Listings) Organic Reach Throttled: Organic search visibility is heavily restricted in favor of Promoted Listings (PL). Sellers who do not pay to promote their items (often at a high ad rate percentage) are finding their items buried under competitors who do. Increased Costs: The shift to "Promoted Listings Advanced" and new attribution models in 2026 means sellers may pay fees on sales not directly driven by ads, eroding margins on high-quality, lower-margin collectibles. 3. Shift in Buyer Behavior and Market Focus Demand for Instant Gratification: Buyers are increasingly filtering by speed (1-day handling) and guaranteed delivery. Collectibles, which are often sold by hobbyists with slower, meticulous shipping, may not meet these high-speed expectations. Shift from Collectibles to Commodities: eBay has shifted its focus to compete with Amazon/Walmart, bringing in an influx of new, low-cost goods that push unique or "pre-owned" collectibles down in search rankings. Pricing "Trust Bands": The algorithm now evaluates if your price fits within a "trust band" (a range of the 30-day median sale price). Even if an item is accurately priced, being outside this band can trigger invisibility. 4. High-Quality Sellers are Targeted by Scammers 100% Feedback as a Target: Paradoxically, a 100% feedback score makes a seller a prime target for scammers who believe they are less likely to fight negative feedback. Return Policy Abuse: As eBay shifts towards "managed returns," sellers often find themselves in situations where they must accept returns for items that were clearly described, yet are penalized in "seller defect" metrics if they fight it.
title: For those who wonder about slow sales.....

text:
No lookers, no watchers, no sales for a month on all items strange/return requests/strange tracking

Had anyone else been seeing a pattern where sales come in batches? Listing on 8 accounts at work across 40 categories or more, and lately I've noticed that sales go dead all at once and come alive all at once. Looking at the counters and watchers and hit counts will jump, a bunch of items will sell, then nothing for a month, its not one item, its not one category, its everything. when there are no sales, there's no hits at all. If we pay for extras, the hit count goes up but still no change in the pattern, but less sales. We sell liquidation items, mostly vintage inventory, mostly sporting goods, collectibles, and parts in general. Other formats don't have this pattern only eBay. Something else that's been happening a lot is buyers putting in for a refund or NAD but canceling the return request after 48 hours. This has happened 9 times this week, on unrelated items, all items where we sold several in the past with no complaints. They file a NAD, then no contact, then 24hrs later closed or dropped? All were low feedback buyers, 0 to 5 FB ratings. All of the items involved here are static items, things that really can't be NAD, new old stock metal parts that are listed by a part number and a picture, all have been items that sold in quantity over and over in the past as we liquidate cases of them, a few to the same buyers in the past. All were low dollar valuve items, nothing over $40 or so. The items haven't changed nor has the shipping method and many of the same buyers have bought the same item again and again. When I messaged to see what was wrong they never replied and the return was 'closed by buyer'. Each one was closed exactly 48 hrs after it was filed with zero communication from the buyer. These all show in our history though even though no actual return or refund ever happened. It seems like just after one of these return requests there's a long lull in buyers and views. We have never had a negative on any account, and never had a return or had to issue a refund but our history says there's been 41? There were no chargebacks to any of the accounts. I watch that closely, always have. Every time someone files for a return is shows a return even though it was canceled. I was told 'That's just how the system works'. I've also noticed a rash of wrong addresses on buyer labels. they buy the item, the label gets printed then before its delivered we get an email saying its being returned due to 'No such Address" but it never comes back? We have 3 that were shipped the first week in Jan. that are still shown bouncing around the country city to city after not being able to be delivered? In each case the address, when I went to Google Maps was one or two numbers higher or lower than the street actually had, usually a dead end and the numbers didn't go that high or that low.
title: No lookers, no watchers, no sales for a month on all items strange/return requests/strange tracking

text:
Buyer filed chargeback for wrong item, bank sided with buyer – no seller protection applied

I wanted to share a recent experience and ask if other sellers have encountered something similar. I sold a character gift set consisting of plush toys and small collectibles. The buyer filed a payment dispute through their financial institution with the reason: “This device does not monitor or test glucose levels as it claims in the product ad.” My listing is clearly for a plush toy / collectible set, not a medical device, and makes no such claims. After the dispute was opened, the buyer returned a pulse oximeter to my home address. This is not an item I sell and is not what was in the order. It appears the buyer disputed the wrong transaction and returned an unrelated item. I responded to the dispute on time and submitted evidence, including photos of the packed order showing the correct items shipped. Despite this, the bank ruled in the buyer’s favor. eBay informed me that the appeal and seller protection review are final and that they cannot reverse the decision or reimburse me. As a result, I lost the sale amount and was also charged a dispute fee. This has been very discouraging for me as a small seller. I also contacted the buyer, but have not received any response. I understand that banks have final authority on chargebacks, but I’m struggling to understand why Seller Protection was not applied in a case where: • The buyer disputed the wrong transaction • The buyer returned a completely different item • The dispute reason does not match the listing • I responded on time and provided evidence Has anyone experienced a chargeback where the buyer disputed the wrong transaction and seller protection was still denied? Is there any further escalation path within eBay for cases like this, or is the decision truly final even when the buyer’s claim is factually unrelated to the listing? I would really appreciate hearing from other sellers or moderators who have dealt with similar situations.
title: Buyer filed chargeback for wrong item, bank sided with buyer – no seller protection applied

Once I successfully contacted customer service by phone, the representative I spoke with was professional and helpful, explained what may have occurred, and advised me on next steps. I followed that guidance carefully and acted in good faith at all times. Unfortunately, due to the duplicate listings: - I was forced into a position where I could not fulfill multiple auctions for one item - I ultimately sold a bundle with a fair market value of approximately $490 for $200 - This resulted in an estimated loss of approximately $290, despite no fault of my own ### Itemized Breakdown of the Bundle (Conservative Values): - 2 oz .999 Silver “No Prey No Pay” Round – approx. $200 - Australian Koala Silver Coin (1 oz, graded) – approx. $110 - Australian Kangaroo Silver Coin (1 oz, graded) – approx. $100 - 1913 Barber Silver Half Dollar (graded) – approx. $15 - ICG-graded Gold Nugget (0.36g) – approx. $65 Total estimated value: ~$490 I am a licensed seller, operate professionally, and take my reputation very seriously. text:
Sending offers Suggestion

this is a suggestion for eBay that i have submitted to eBay for several years to no success. (i have also suggested the send offer function for years before it was finally implemented.) the main idea is for sellers to be able to send offers to 2nd -6th-ish highest bid for a past auctions. I find this to be one of the biggest barriers to entry for regular people selling one off items or used items. for example a guy with rare old collectible tool that they don't make anymore, he wants to get rid of it so he looks up the tool on eBay and sees one sold at auction 8 months ago for 100$, and there were 10 bids on it and 2nd place bid was for 90$. So he would send a picture and offer of his tool to the 2nd place bidder for 90$. and so on with the lower place bidders depending on how low he would be willing go. it could even be a higher than 90$ offer if he thinks his tool is in better condition. the problem is for the bidders on rare or unusual items. if they lost, and these things only pop up once or twice a year they could give up and not be actively looking when the next one comes around. this also works out for the seller who wants to sell something obscure, but is unmotivated to actually go through the hassle of making the listing because he knows a weird once a year item doesn't have a strong chance of those same bidders finding his listing. but if he has a way to send an offer to the bidders directly then he knows he has a better chance to sell. this could work just as well for buy it now items and is especially true of industrial items. for example a building owner has a breaker go bad, but its an old breaker that isn't made any more and he gets real lucky and finds the only one listed on ebay and buys at a "buy it now" price. then 6 months later he gets an offer for the same breaker at a reduced price, so he buys it as a spare. obviously this doesn't work for the mass produced china stuff but for used, industrial, obscure, and especially for collectibles this would turn every loser bid or buy it now item in to a "looking for add" that sellers could see and reach out with offers. of course this function would need to be something the bidders could opt into or turn off completely otherwise the the volume china sellers would bombard people with crap, and also you would be keeping bidders anonymous. basically when you lose bidding on a 1984 bob ross lunch box, you get asked if you would like to receive offers from sellers with the same or similar items. and then a week later you get an offer for a 1986 bob ross lunch box, for your bid price. I don't know, I've got lots of ideas on how to put guardrails on this thing, but in general I think this would be a good idea. Any thoughts on why this wouldn't work?
title: Sending offers Suggestion

Goings book Mammy and Uncle Mose: Black Collectibles and American Stereotyping (Bloomington: Indiana Univ. Press, 1994). text:
Clarification needed: Vintage perfume miniatures (collectibles) & locked violations

Hi all — hoping someone can clarify how eBay applies its policy here, as I’m getting inconsistent explanations from support. I sell vintage perfume miniatures as collectibles , not for use. The items are approx. 40–100 years old, discontinued, many boxed, transparently described and photographed. They are listed in Collectibles → Collectible Miniature Perfumes , not in cosmetics or fragrance categories. I’ve been selling them for ~2 months with good feedback. Recently, around 20% of my listings were removed under the Perfume & Cosmetics policy. From what I can tell, this affects mainly bottles that are not 100% full, but also includes some empty display pieces and some full boxed items. This is where I’m struggling to understand the policy application: Partial fill levels and collectibles Vintage perfume naturally evaporates over decades, even if unopened. Among collectors, this is expected and does not affect collectability. If any non-100% fill level makes an item prohibited, it seems that older (and therefore more collectable) miniatures would automatically be excluded — which makes the existence of a “collectible miniature perfumes” category unclear. Is there a specific definition eBay uses to distinguish collectible miniatures from perfume for use ? Policy transparency / enforcement I’ve spoken to support but haven’t been able to get a clear explanation of what is actually allowed for collectible miniatures, especially older ones. Is there guidance somewhere that addresses age, evaporation, sealed vs unsealed, or display-only items? Locked violations / appeals I can see the “Start appeal” and “Remove listing” buttons for the affected listings, but they’re not clickable (cursor shows 🚫 ). Is this a temporary lock during review, or does it indicate something else? I’m trying to understand what I should change to stay compliant going forward. Any insight from experienced sellers or moderators would be much appreciated. Thanks in advance.
title: Clarification needed: Vintage perfume miniatures (collectibles) & locked violations

Using ebay suggested pricing on Manage Active Listings page

It does this very quickly and I assume an AI is coming up with the number. In an effort to move old inventory I tried adjusting many items and I am not positive it has made a difference. text:
Ebay's Return Policy Favors Fraudulent Coin Dealers and Good Knockoffs

Ebay should make exceptions on return time limits for coins because it takes much more than what is now ruled to verify a coin by a trustworthy third party like PCGS. Here is the story. I participated in an auction for a lot of several coins last October (2025) and won. Then, I sent one of the coins to be graded by PCGS. For those who are not familiar with coin grading, it usually takes more than a month for a coin to come back after submission. Much to my disappointment and astonishment, the coin that I regarded very legit turned out to be a counterfeit. However, due to the time limit of ebay's return window, I could virtually do nothing but message the seller about this. He said that he only served as a middleman that holds auctions for the consignors, and he would only make 10% of the proceeds. Now that it was long past the return window, the consignor already took the money, and thus there was no fix for it. He "kindlessly" offered to refund only his share of the proceeds, but I declined. Would it be the real business model for auction hosts to take advantage of ebay's return & refund policy to reap money from collectors with high-end knockoffs?
title: Ebay's Return Policy Favors Fraudulent Coin Dealers and Good Knockoffs

text:
counterfeit coins

I've been a collector for 55 years, and have bought lots of coins and even sold a few on eBay. I am extremely disturbed by what I've been seeing for the past several years, just a few at first, but now some so extreme that they feature banner headlines and photographs on the home page. I'm talking about the scourge of phony coins, most of them from sellers with very low feedback numbers, usually not in the US. I'm seeing key dates being offered for a fraction of market value, in virtually uncirculated condition, and available in quantity. None of these coins are marked as copies (a U.S. law), nor does the listing say they are copies or any other euphemism for being just plain fake. I'm an experienced collector and can usually spot them, but there are many newer collectors out there that see these 'rare' coins at unbelievably low prices and buy them. Then the whole cascade of bad stuff starts to happen--rejection by a 3rd party grading service or a reputable dealer; family members who many years from now think they've inherited gems lying ungraded in folders when what they have turns out to be junk. Another problem is the newer advent of listing authentic coins that aren't worth much more than face value but creating a descriptive narrative suggesting they are The Holy Grail. A normal bicentennial quarter or a 1957-D penny snatched out of circulation is listed for $800 and is seen in glorious color on the home page. Surely it must be special and valuable if it's there, right? I'm becoming increasingly hesitant to bid for coins on eBay unless they are slabbed and I can verity them with the grading service, but with the fraud with slabs that has been invading the rare sports card market, even that is no guarantee. Raw coins are just too risky. Silver Eagles are occasionally offered for less than spot value but have a strange attraction to magnets (I have 2 of those and they look legit). I've tried to flag auctions and notify eBay, but the few times I've tried, they did nothing and even went so far as to say that they found no problem or evidence of fraud. Their tolerance and complacency are contributing to the demise of this popular hobby. I would encourage others who see obvious fraud to report it, not just glance over it. Maybe they can't ignore us forever.
title: counterfeit coins

text:
feedback outside window

I buy coins on ebay. Some of the coins are expensive. I try to only buy from sellers with a lot of positive feedback. Some of these sellers have thousands of positive feedback. The problem is this. When I receive a coin, if it is valuable I send it to PCGS to be graded. The grading process takes over 40 days to be completed. Many times, when I receive my grade, the coin comes back as cleaned or grades much lower than described. The window for leaving feedback has closed. I can no longer leave negative feedback. Or return the coin since the return window has also closed. Some sellers do offer a refund minus ebay fees and shipping. Some sellers reply rudely, some not at all. This is a problem because I feel some of these sellers have inflated feedback ratings because the window for leaving feedback is closed. So, they show hundreds of positive feedback but no negative. Since they have so many positive feedbacks other buyers think they are safe to buy from. They aren't, but they aren't getting the neutral and negative feedback they deserve. I have lost thousands buying coins on ebay. What can I do about this?
title: feedback outside window

text:
Auto-Closed INAD Return After Grading Delay – Stuck in Loop, No Appeal Button. Workarounds?

Hi eBay Community, Frustrated long-time buyer here, 100% feedback over 25 years. Need advice on appealing an auto-closed INAD case—the site loops me to the "Return Closed" page with no "Appeal this decision" button, even though it's within 30 days of closure. Bought raw trading cards listed as "near mint or better" in a combined shipment. Card looked great by naked eye and novice magnification (matching description). Submitted to third-party grading service right after delivery (took 6+ weeks to process). The card was returned "Authentic-Altered" due to an undisclosed mechanical alteration (e.g., edge rolling, only detectable under professional magnification). No numerical grade, making the card all but worthless. Contacted seller twice politely for refund + return label; they refused, noting no grade guarantee (understandable, but undisclosed alteration = INAD). Opened INAD same day received slabbed card (over the weekend) via automated link—auto-closed immediately for timing (past 30-day guarantee from delivery). Tried appealing via Purchase History > order details: Loops back, no options. Called support multiple times in the last few weekdays: Automated system only, will not allow human transfer. Chat unavailable or loops too. Requesting extension for grading delay (fits "limited circumstances" in collectibles policy?). Never ran into this problem before. Altering a card is a major seller deception and destroys buyer confidence. Need advice on getting to the bottom of this. Bypass loop for appeal with new evidence (grading label/photos/notes, messages, timeline)? Force human escalation (tier 2 rep)? Start a new "Ask eBay to step in" or report problem? Evidence prepped. Willing to return with prepaid label. Thanks for practical tips—done with the runaround! Thank you
title: Auto-Closed INAD Return After Grading Delay – Stuck in Loop, No Appeal Button. Workarounds?

text:
Grading Condition Definitions

Ebay is NOT a grading company and the definitions they have for sports cards do not represent actual conditions of listed cards. PSA is the standard for grading. This needs to be addressed as buyers are once again taking advantage of sellers based on what eBays definitions say. My 2 cents PSA grading scale definitions Grade Name Key Characteristics 10 Gem Mint Virtually perfect condition with flawless centering, corners, surface, and edges. 9 Mint Superb condition with extremely minor flaws, such as a minor printing imperfection or slight off-centering. 8 Near Mint-Mint Excellent condition, appearing near-mint at first glance, but may have slight corner fraying or minor printing imperfections. 7 Near Mint Slight surface wear visible upon close inspection, with slight fraying on some corners and potential minor printing blemishes. 6 Excellent-Mint Shows more visible surface wear, more visible corner rounding, or a light scratch. 5 Excellent Shows more wear than a PSA 8, with more rounded corners, surface wear, or light scuffs. 4 Very Good-Excellent Shows clear signs of wear, with rounded corners and surface wear, scuffs, or scratches. 3 Very Good Corners are more rounded, with surface wear and noticeable scuffs or scratches. 2 Good Corners are visibly rounded, and surface wear is obvious. There may be scratching, light staining, or scuffing. 1.5 Fair Extreme corner wear, surface scuffs, and staining. May have a heavy crease, but the card must be fully intact. 1 Poor Exhibits major damage, such as missing pieces, major creasing, extreme discoloration, or warping. Additional grades and qualifiers Authentic ( NO 😞 Cards are authenticated but not graded numerically, often due to being in too poor a condition to grade. Authentic Altered ( AA 😞 Cards that are genuine but have been altered in some way, such as having corners repaired or colors enhanced. Qualifiers: PSA may add qualifiers to a grade to indicate specific issues, such as OC for "Off Center" or ST for "Staining".
title: Grading Condition Definitions

text:
Need guidance for no fault returns and the path forward

@eastlaneave Thank you for your other reply. I tried to reply to the thread itself but I guess I can't reply there either so I started a new thread here. I had also posted in another forum before I saw your tag so I apologize, this is double posted, or maybe triple if my reply to the other thread is just hidden from my view. Anyway, this is my situation. In late September ebay removed the "just didn't like it" buyers remorse return reason. For collectable sellers this is akin to taking away "doesn't fit" from clothing sellers. Now buyers are left with 2 options. Option 1 take responsibility, or Option 2 Blame the seller. No reason exists unless the buyer wants to say they changed their mind or bought by mistake. No more no fault "just didn't like it" where the buyer got an accurately described item that just isn't to their taste. No one did anything wrong the buyer just didn't like it. As a certified coin seller my items are all authenticated and graded by an ebay approved grading service that ebay has approved by name in their currency policy, and they outline very stringent standards (examples the service must employ a rich PNG member and prove your net worth to be eligible, and have professionally graded more than 50,000 coins, include a financial guarantees of grade and authenticity etc) . In other words, if I ship the pictured item it can't be SNAD. But buyers sometimes disagree with the grade and just don't like it but are forced to admit fault when they were not at fault, or blame the blameless seller. This would be the same thing as a SNAD return from a clothing seller if they removed "doesn't fit". Everyone would say "not as described - says medium but seems too large". Can anyone tag a mod for guidance on this or pass of the relevant teams? This really was a major oversight or maybe even glitch. Any guidance on this major problem would be greatly appreciated!
title: Need guidance for no fault returns and the path forward

text:
Introducing Ron Jaiven: eBay’s new US General Manager

Hello sellers, Thank you to everyone who joined last week’s Winter Seller Check-In. It was great to see and hear from our eBay community. If you couldn’t join, you can watch the full session on YouTube . Below is a brief introduction to me and the areas where I’ll be focused. I’m Ron Jaiven, the new General Manager for eBay North America. I have the great privilege of continuing the great momentum of Dawn Block, who led this seller community for the past 18 months. I joined eBay in 2013 and have worked in many roles across eBay. Most recently, I was the General Manager of our US Collectibles category, where I launched new enthusiast experiences such as Grading Add-On services for trading cards. Prior to that, I served as General Manager for our US Parts & Accessories category (P&A) where I helped release our Guaranteed Fit program and launch several marketing campaigns. And previously, I led our US Vehicles category, where I relaunched our eBay Motors mobile app. I also had roles in our eBay Classifieds Group, served as Chief of Staff to the eBay CEO, and worked in our Corporate Strategy department. And now I’m now excited to bring these experiences to help support our sellers across eBay. Sellers will remain central to my work in this new role. You are entrepreneurial, committed to your businesses and to the eBay community, and you use this marketplace to create economic opportunity for yourselves and others. I have deep respect for that. My first priority is to support growth for the eBay community. Strong sellers lead to strong buyer demand and a healthy platform. I look forward to a steady, practical partnership—listening carefully, learning from your expertise, and acting on what matters most. Your voice will continue to guide our decisions, and your feedback will inform how we evolve the eBay platform in 2026 and beyond. It’s an exciting time at eBay, we just celebrated 30 years as a company and we’re looking forward to what’s ahead. I can’t wait to share more insights and answer your questions as we all prepare for peak season. Stay tuned in the community to hear more from me in the next month. Thanks, Ron Jaiven, Vice President & General Manager, eBay North America
title: Introducing Ron Jaiven: eBay’s new US General Manager

text:
“Sign In With Apple” on eBay App Prevents Vault Access

When you purchase a graded collectible on eBay in the PSA vault, and you do not currently have a vault linked to your account, it will create one for you from your current eBay email. The problem is if you use “Sign in with Apple” login for the eBay app, which creates a private relay address, the PSA account is created in this email, and this locks your purchase behind an account you literally cannot access. Private relay works by only allowing emails from approved domains. Because eBay has not worked with PSA to allow them to send emails to private relay users, we cannot get the registration email, and unable to access purchases. This is not an “apple issue”, their platform fully supports this , it just seems there is no currently no implementation on the eBay app. Currently, the only solution to this is to contact PSA to have them change the email on the account for you. This requires sending copies of your ids among other things, and currently they have over a 20 business day turnaround time due to the market booming. eBay is providing a very poor experience for first time buyers and delaying customers from getting their hands on orders PSA is having to manually verify IDs, and have customer service manually merge or change the “bad account”. Both companies must be spending time on the phones dealing with customer frustration about this. A few weeks of one time developer work would allow customers true ownership of their purchases quicker, save eBay/PSA on CS costs, and provide a seamless experience to users. ______ Am I missing an obvious solution? I find plenty of others in this same scenario with a simple google search, are we just misinformed? Does eBay just not think it’s an issue on their side ? PSA is their partner, I didn’t expect to have to wait well over a month to even have something in a vault I can access.
title: “Sign In With Apple” on eBay App Prevents Vault Access

text:
PSA Cards Attached to Apple Relay Email Unaccessable

When a PSA vault card is purchased on eBay, it is sent to the email address associated with the eBay account. A PSA account is created with the email on file for the eBay account. If you are using an Apple private relay email; you will never be able to login to the PSA account, as the two factor email will never make it to your inbox. If PSA is a official eBay partner, they should be marked as an approved sending domain so users can receive password resets/2FA codes from them. PSA is backed up and taking over a month to resolve vault transfers. eBay should simply add the PSA emails to the approved Apple sending domains, rather than making items inaccessible and saying “you have to talk to PSA”. Why hasn’t this happened? Is there a technical reason? It doesn’t make sense for them to be an official partner, but not be allowed to contact the relay email.
title: PSA Cards Attached to Apple Relay Email Unaccessable

text:
EBAY IS SCAMMING EVERYONE

Ebays deal with GIA is to authenticate fine jewelry has been widely reported. Industry defines fine jewelry as jewelry incorporating precious metal and or gems, I think everyone is clear on that. And this is exactly why there is an issue with Ebays Authenticity Program I sold a “costume”/ “fashion” necklace on Ebay for $675. I was required to send it to be authenticated. Costume jewelry is inconsistently selected for authentication many items over 500 that don't show that requirement. I called Ebay many times and was repeatedly told GIA was the authenticator. The industry wide understanding of “costume” or “fashion” jewelry is that it is made of brass white metal and or other non precious metals, often electroplated with gold, silver or rhodium, it may or may not incorporate other non-precious materials. So obviously there is NOTHING for GIA to authenticate!!! Explanations of the Authentication program given by Customer Support have been laughable in their inconsistency and lack of understanding. A an obvious indicator of a lack of training and poor corporate communication. Several customer service reps said don't mention gold, white gold or rhodium plating in the listing. I was also told not to list anything under vintage jewelry. So I’m being told not to inform my buyers about materials required by Ebay item specifics to prevent this ridiculous process . Each time Ebay makes an improvement a it inevitably causes far more issues than it addresses. Ebay needs to fix it's poorly designed and completely ineffective authentication program for jewelry. The claim of authenticating costume jewelry is in fact a lie, and only adds more complications and delays to a system burdened by redundancy and useless policy tweaks. .
title: EBAY IS SCAMMING EVERYONE

text:
Seller Search Manipulation: Defective items listed as 'USED' instead of "FOR PARTS OR REPAIR"

I've encountered a disturbing (and alarmingly -- INCREASING ) trend involving sellers violating Ebay's listing practices for defective items. I have noticed this trend has increased dramatically over the past few years. Worse, it is no longer possible to simply and quickly REPORT these fraudulent listings as I find them. Increasingly, Sellers are listing defective items under the ' USED' category that (according to eBay rules) must be listed as "For Parts or Repair". This is bad for everyone -- there are a number of problematic effects on Buyers, AND on Sellers AND on Ebay. Examples: 1) NEGATIVE IMPACT ON LEGITIMATE SELLERS: A defective item deceptively listed as USED sells for a fraction of what it's value as a WORKING item would be. This depresses the value of WORKING items corrctly listed as used. 2) NEGATIVE FINANCIAL IMPACT ON EBAY: As this deception becomes more commonly used, the result is that Ebay's pricing data (sold items) becomes polluted to the point of being untrustworthy. This drives down selling prices of WORKING items, and reduces Ebay's Seller Fees . 3) NEGATIVE REPUTATIONAL IMPACT ON EBAY: When the 'sold' pricing trends for properly listed items includes 'data pollution' from defective items sold cheaply under the used category, Sellers can no longer price their items to true USED market value for NON-DEFECTIVE items. Moreover, when Ebay's search filters are used (and FAIL) to eliminate non-working items from search results, Ebay starts to feel like a market that is friendly to and TOLERANT of scammers. 4) ILLICIT BENEFITS TO DISHONEST SELLERS: A dishonest seller, by simply disclosing some kind of non-working behavior deeply buried in the description, or even using the excuse that they were unable to test the item, dishonest sellers can defeat Ebay's return guarantee no matter what defects were actually present. 5) NEGATIVE IMPACT ON BUYERS: As illicit sellers increasingly list defective items in the (fully operational) USED category, Buyers can no longer trust that items listed as USED meet Ebay's carefully and explicitly worded criteria for CONDITION = " Used: An item that has been used previously. The item may have some signs of cosmetic wear, but is fully operational and functions as intended ." I've been buying and selling on Ebay since 1999. I've never seen Sellers abusing and manipulating Ebay's search systems on this scale before. When shopping I now spend hours sorting through USED listings to sort out these defective items for searches that used to only take minutes. In the past, I could EASILY report these listings but Ebay has removed (or effectively hidden) any ability to report these deceptive (and arguably fraudulent) listings. Moreover, on the several occasions where I've been 'bitten', I wound up in ugly, time-consuming 'litigation' with Ebay customer service in trying to get a refund. EBAY -- YOU ARE DRIVING CUSTOMER...
title: Seller Search Manipulation: Defective items listed as 'USED' instead of "FOR PARTS OR REPAIR"

text:
eBay guarantee is a joke. Scammed on eBay with undeniable proof

eBay has undeniable proof that I was scammed by a seller along with many other buyers of the same. so the item was a Leopold five HD rifle scope worth approximately $1300 so here’s what the seller is doing. He is copying and pasting old listings that have previously sold on eBay at normal prices in duplicating them at a discounted price point. I knew right away. It was a scam whenever the seller never responded after I purchased the item contacted eBay immediately they said they could not do anything until I receive the item. the item was marked as delivered, but I have unreusable evidence that UPS never arrived at my location. I also have undeniable proof that UPS supplied me showing that the matching tracking number from the item that I purchased was delivered at an alternate address, but in the same city as myself and the same ZIP Code. eBay was supplied to all the evidence and still refused to refund me be aware the 100% guarantee from eBay is a joke. They told me to get the police involved, but how do I get the police involved for an item that was never delivered so it obviously was not stolen. Could not file a claim with UPS because the actual shipping label was created using an Amazon account. so now I have been on the phone for five hours now and recently just got off the phone with Amazon and was able to track down the tracking number that was attached to a stud finder that was sold. so the seller is using his Amazon profile to author street addresses uploading the information to eBay so that it will show delivered even though it wasn’t delivered to that person’s door..
title: eBay guarantee is a joke. Scammed on eBay with undeniable proof

text:
eBay Authentication Process - Untrustworthy?

Sellers, I’ve been on eBay for 19 years and have maintained a Top Seller rating the entire time. In nearly two decades, I’ve never had a serious issue beyond a couple of routine returns. I go above and beyond to protect myself as a seller, communicate clearly, and ensure my listings are accurate and transparent. Unfortunately, this past month has been a very different experience and a very concerning one. I sold a very rare vintage jewelry piece for $4,900. Every detail was carefully documented in the listing, including accurate measurements and clear photos to avoid any confusion about authenticity or size. The item was sent to eBay’s Authenticity Guarantee program. However, because it was categorized as a pendant instead of a charm, they declined to authenticate it even though the size and details were clearly disclosed. Despite this, the item was still shipped to the buyer. After receiving it, the buyer claimed it was not in the correct category (pendant vs. charm), which is a very minor distinction and did not affect the item itself. Nonetheless, eBay allowed the return. The item was shipped back to the authenticator, where it sat for nearly a week, and then with the USPS for over a week, then finally returned to me in a box taped shut. When I opened the package, the pendant was missing! You can image how that felt. I immediately contacted eBay, documented everything with photos, and opened a case. Now I’m waiting and extremely concerned. Not only am I potentially out nearly $5,000, but I may also be responsible for shipping costs as well. At this point, I don’t know where the failure occurred. It could have been an error during handling at eBay’s Authenticity Guarantee facility where the item was not properly repackaged, or potentially an issue during transit with USPS. Either way, the item was present when originally shipped and is now missing during a process that was entirely outside of my control. As a seller of 19 years with a flawless track record, this is incredibly stressful and disheartening. Situations like this highlight how much risk sellers carry, even when we follow every guideline and do everything right. It feels like the policies heavily favor buyers, leaving sellers exposed in cases like this. If this isn’t resolved fairly, I’ll have to seriously reconsider continuing to sell high value items on eBay. The risk is simply too great. I’ve always trusted the platform, but experiences like this make it much harder to confidently do business here. Has anyone else experienced something similar, particularly involving the Authenticity Guarantee program and returns? Any advice would be greatly appreciated.
title: eBay Authentication Process - Untrustworthy?

The next morning, there was an eBay notice that the Seller, indeed, issued a refund for the full price of the item - $275!!! I message the Seller to ask, "How do you make any money in this deal?" But I haven't gotten a reply. What's the scam? I am waiting for the refund to process & get applied to my credit card before I go to the P.O. to see what the item looks like...maybe it's an empty box? I just don't get it. Considering eBay's Money Back Guarantee, how could this be profitable for a scammer? The Seller has a good rating with lots of sales and decent reviews. Some of the reviews mention the same situation happening to them, but they go on to say that the item worked out perfectly for them. This whole thing is crazy weird. Any thoughts?

text:
Buyer claims “Item not as described”

Looking to get opinions on a “item not as described” case that was just opened against me.. I sold a set of diesel fuel injectors on here and I put in the description: “90% positive that these injectors are good and could be installed today and the truck would run fine, but I cannot guarantee” and “they have been sitting on a shelf in a box for 2 years” the buyer received my shipment at 10am on 2/23/26 and started the claim at 9pm the same day (which if you know about changing diesel injectors, it is possible that he was able to change them out in this amount of time, but unlikely.) And included a picture of his code reader showing only 1 out of 8 injectors balance rate out of manufacture specifications. The other 7 were fine. eBay automatically accepted his return on my behalf and now I have to wait for him to ship them back and how do I know he’s not going to change them all out and send me back his old injectors that were giving him issues? Or what if he just sends me back with a box full of lead and keeps all the injectors?! How do you prove that to eBay? and on another note I sold them to him for the same price that a set of 8 just bad/rebuildable core injectors sell for so I was basically just selling them as a set of rebuildable cores, not as a set of good injectors because I didn’t know if they were good or not. A set of known-good injectors sell for twice as much or more than what I sold these for. Do I ask eBay to step in and look at my description where I say I cannot guarantee? do I just wait for the return to get sent back to me, make sure it’s the correct item, refund the money, and repost them with a more detailed description? im not worried about the money and if I get them back I know I can resell them for the same amount or more than I did selling them as just a set of not working injectors/good rebuildable core injectors, im more worried about not getting back the same items I shipped or them shipping a worthless item back in a box and me having to issue a refund of $500 All this buyer protection stuff in place, where’s the seller protection?! Seems like eBay always sides with the buyer without even giving us sellers a chance to defend ourself or share our side of the story. any advise will be greatly appreciated thanks

text:
eBay authenticity center sends my sneakers to wrong address… proceeds to tell me, “kick rocks.”

I sold a pair of sneakers through eBay’s authentication program. You know — the premium, white-glove, “trust the system” pipeline. Shoes go to the authentication hub. They inspect them. If they pass, great. If they fail, they’re shipped back to me. These failed inspection. Fine. Send them home. Except… they never came home. eBay tells me they shipped the sneakers to the “return address on file.” The address they used is an old one — ancient history. My default address in the system is my current address, and I’ve successfully received authenticated returns there before. No issues. No confusion. No mystery. But this time? The system decided to time-travel. Now the sneakers are gone. No delivery to my current residence. No proof of delivery provided to me. No package at my door. No postal confirmation. No Ring footage. Nothing. It’s been over a month. At this point, this isn’t “in transit.” This is “missing in action.” Let’s review the chain of custody like grown adults: • The sneakers were under eBay’s authentication hub control. • I didn’t change or redirect any address mid-process. • I’ve previously received authenticated returns correctly. • I do not have the sneakers. • I do not have the money. Yet the current solution is essentially: “We’re monitoring it.” Monitoring what? A ghost? If this were a buyer case and there was no verifiable proof of delivery, a refund would be issued quickly under the Money Back Guarantee. That standard exists. It’s clear. It works. But because this shipment originated inside the authentication process, suddenly we’re in bureaucratic purgatory. I’ve requested two simple outcomes: 1 Refund the amount owed. 2 Replace the item with one of equal value. I’m not asking for damages. I’m not asking for interest. I’m asking for parity. Right now I’m out both merchandise and funds due to a failure that occurred entirely within eBay’s controlled system. That shouldn’t require a month of escalation. So I’m turning to the community: Has anyone dealt with authenticated returns being shipped to an outdated address despite having a correct default address? What escalation channel actually results in resolution when the authentication hub is involved? Is there a formal path beyond social media and standard support loops? This shouldn’t be complicated. If the system loses an item, the system makes it right. I just need the system to act like the billion-dollar machine it claims to be.
title: eBay authenticity center sends my sneakers to wrong address… proceeds to tell me, “kick rocks.”

Authenticity Guarantee Fails ANOTHER sold piece...for the SECOND time!

We are a very reputable vintage jewelry shop with Ebay since 2006. We have had several items fail the Authenticity Guarantee because of metal karat weight...and it is LUDICROUS. That they have the ability to "put fear into our buyers" over non industry standard reasons is maddening! We have lost over $5,000 for this reason and that is so damaging to small, family businesses in these economic times! Today, the "authenticators" are calling a stamped and signed Barry B. 950 Silver ring as "closer to 925" and therefore failing the ring!! The backstory on this ring is that it has failed authentication TWICE NOW!!! Both times to the same buyer. The first time it was for the "Black Stones" that we had listed as "Black Onyx" as B.B. uses this stone most often. Because of the way they were set, this was as close of an ID as we could get. They came back stating they were "Black Spinel" a MORE valuable stone by about $5. They told us to just list them in the future as "Black Stones" to avoid any further Authenticity failings. Same buyer bought the ring again and THIS TIME it failed for its Silver content! When in FACT it is 950 silver because THAT IS WHAT B.B. USES!!! FURTHERMORE...the buyer happens to work at a jewelry store and LITERALLY SPOKE TO B.B. on the phone and he himself verified that is the silver weight he typically uses! Because of this phone conversation...when Ebay called our buyer to ask if he still wanted the ring, he accepted the ring. Just to put some perspective on the difference between 925 and 950. The actual difference in silver content between 925 and 950 in this ring is less than a gram (about $2.30 with today’s crazy high silver prices, and around 32 cents worth when the ring was originally made), and the discrepancy is likely because of B.B.'s scale being slightly off when he was measuring out the metal mixture before casting. While GIA is a rockstar in the world of gemstone identification, their idealized standards for jewelry are out of touch with the imperfect nature of jewelry making, and don’t reflect the industry accepted variations that are expected in every piece (from antiques to modern name brands). Today our buyer let me know that he received the ring and is BLOWN AWAY by how amazing it was and can't believe he almost missed out on this ring AFTER TWO FAILED AUTHENTICATIONS by GIA! He also stated that he has had SEVERAL items (from other sellers) fail that he wasn't able to receive because of these NEGLIGENT actions taken by Ebays Authenticity group there at GIA. IN FACT...once he received the ring, he ran it thru his jewelry stores XRF machine and it stated that it was a SOLID 950! This is so infuriating on so many levels: 1. It makes us look like NON-reputable sellers when that is the furthest thing from the truth 2. We have no way of responding other than emailing our buyer and pleading our case. But we look terrible in their eyes already because...
title: Authenticity Guarantee Fails ANOTHER sold piece...for the SECOND time!

However when my product arrived it was very dirty, and had glaring malfunctions, screen had so much yellowing it looked like it came from a chronic smoker's house, and most importantly various buttons (3 of them) didn't work. When I shared this with the seller I got the offer of a partial refund of 20 USD if I left positive feedback first, or send it back for a full refund. I have looked around for info on how I could proceed and help for a fairer solution since I know locking a partial refund like that is not allowed, and everything leads me to start a refund process. However since I've had to pay VAT for customs, I prefer to keep the item and repair it myself as otherwise that VAT would be as good as thrown away, and seeing as the seller has already offered it, I would end up losing the item. After explaining my case as best I could while summarized, I want to ask if I should involve eBay support (and in which manner) or something similar due to the seller keeping the partial refund only after my positive feedback has been confirmed, or just try to continue negotiating for a better partial refund (to at least cover the repairs as they need new motherboard and screen replacements, latter coming at very steep prices) that wouldn't involve something unallowed by eBay, also considering the difference between the item description and marketing compared to the item received.

text:
Sellers using fraudulent tracking numbers

Back in november I made a purchase on ebay and within the next day or two the item was marked as shipped but no tracking was provided. The day it was supposed to be delivered I messaged the seller and asked where the package was and why there was no tracking provided. They then changed the shipping details to show a tracking number that stated the item was delivered! I immediately opened a case with ebay and within a few weeks had my money refunded because nothing was ever delivered. Now I'm going through it all over again. This time instead of waiting I messaged the seller [removed by moderator] and asked why there was no tracking number provided. They "apologized" and said they will update the shipping details to show a tracking number and also sent me a picture of a USPS receipt with the same tracking number. I said ok thats fine and then entered the number on the USPS tracking website. What came up was exactly this: 'Tracking is not available for this item. This may be because the tracking number is invalid; USPS has not yet received payment; USPS has not yet received the item; the item does not include tracking; or other reasons.' The buyer had a receipt which meant USPS has the item therefore tracking would've stated that. So they somehow managed to create a picture (ai?) of the receipt which was cropped to only show the section of the receipt that states the destination, price, and tracking, not which USPS location or any other information. If this is the new norm on ebay then count me out. I can't believe ebay hasnt caught on to these scammers. It seems like theres dozens of "new sellers" with low feedback ratings scamming people, yet I feel like im the only one going through it since like I said it seems like ebay hasnt caught on. How can I contact ebay before the so called delivery date and let them know that there is a scam already in process?? Why do I have to wait weeks until I have my money refunded? And why isnt ebay doing anything to prevent these scammers from creating accounts??
title: Sellers using fraudulent tracking numbers

The seller acted like I was lying about not receiving my package and asked me to close the case. Of course this upset me, so I sent him a picture of my house with my address number clearly displayed. When I requested a refund, he had the nerve to photo shop the picture of my house that I sent him with a fake package on the step and said it was delivered. I can tell it was photo shopped because for some reason he added Christmas lights to my banister and removed my door mat? Also, his "photo" was the exact photo I sent him (same angle) with the mentioned alterations. It was also suspicious that the photo was darker as if to conceal some of the editing. I was just wondering if anyone else has experienced this type of behavior and how I can remove this seller from Ebay before others fall prey to his scams? If I put 5 days as handling and no scans for 4 days then I would NOT have gotten the strikes?????????????? In BIN listings, the estimated delivery date is FAR in the future. I looked back on something I just purchased and EBAY apparently changed the original listing date to some crazy far off ETA date. Yet it once said delivery by March 3 (yeah it is lost) BTW changing handling time makes ZERO changes to delivery dates. I just changed something to 5 day handling time and the date a buyer would receive stays same???? Based on this somewhat weird pretzel logic a seller is better off putting a big handling time. Although in reality if I sold something waited 5 days to ship I would think the buyer would be **bleep** 😞

text:
Fraudulent Return not covered by seller protection

After more than 23 years as an eBay seller specializing in electronics, I’m extremely disappointed with how a recent case was handled and I’m hoping someone from eBay support will take a closer look. A buyer returned a graphics card claiming it was defective. However, the unit I received back was clearly tampered with—the serial number had been scratched off, making it impossible to verify that it was the same item originally sent. The item I shipped was brand new and factory sealed. I submitted documentation to support this, including my purchase invoice and evidence showing the item was sealed before shipment. Despite this evidence, my appeal was denied and the buyer was refunded $4,008.26. The explanation I received was that my account had already received two “courtesy credits” under seller protection within the past 12 months. Unfortunately, situations like this are completely outside of a seller’s control. When buyers abuse the return system or commit return fraud, seller protection is supposed to exist to protect legitimate sellers who provide proof. I’m respectfully asking eBay to review this case again. The returned item was tampered with and the serial number was intentionally removed, which strongly suggests fraudulent activity. Sellers who provide clear documentation should not be penalized because they have previously needed to rely on seller protection when dealing with bad actors. I’ve been a loyal seller on this platform for over two decades and have always conducted my business honestly. I hope someone from eBay’s support or trust & safety team can take another look at this case. Thank you. Scratched off serial number returned by customer
title: Fraudulent Return not covered by seller protection

text:
Final Sale Item that doesn't work and isn't in the condition described in the listing!?

OK. I know there's a risk buying something from a seller that doesn't accept returns. But I bought some earbuds on December 31 and received them in early January. They were described as open-box, in excellent, like new condition. And they were great. Before the end of February, one of the earbuds stopped working properly... it's charge time diminished over a week or so, until it only lasted 90 minutes; the other one was only down to 70%. The case was still fully charged. I cleaned all of the contacts, did a hard reset on them, unpaired and re-paired them a few times, and charged them with different USB cables. I reached out to the seller for help, but didn't get a response; so a week later, I reached out again. I received a screenshot of the manufacturer's self-help tips, which I'd already seen. I tried everything one more time with no luck. I reached out again, providing screenshots, and I haven't heard back. And now, I can't figure out how to get any help from eBay about this issue, the website keeps taking me in circles and telling me "the return window has closed". What? What else should I do? Apparently I threw money down the drain, which is a shame.
title: Final Sale Item that doesn't work and isn't in the condition described in the listing!?

text:
Would you block this guy?

Hey gang, So I have this buyer who purchased and received two shirts, one black and one blue. Both size medium. They messaged me saying I sent them the wrong size. The black one they received is size large. The blue one is fine (medium), but I sent them the wrong size for black and need a replacement or refund. I always ask for a photo confirmation for issues like this. So I did. Sidenote: honestly in this day and age I don't know why buyers would even bother to send a message like that without an accompanying photo. Doesn't ebay even now require a photo to be attached to not as described returns? I forget if that policy has flip-flopped back to unnecessary anymore. Anyway after a few days of silence, the buyer responded with "Oh it turns out the black is a medium. When I got the package I was in a rush between coming back from work and little league practice and had to get my kid ready and out the door since we were running late, I didnt read the tag correctly and everything was all chaotic and in a hurry. When I came back to take the photo I notice that both shirts are in fact medium. Once I slowed down and looked at the tags again. Again, I apologize for the inconvenience." I didn't block them frankly because they didn't make me lose any money for their boneheadedness. But I know how many sellers don't even bother to ask for a photo and simply tell them to return for refund. Had I done this, I would've likely processed a return/refund for no reason and lost out on shipping or a sale. I just wanted to know if others would block this buyer for the inconvenience. I'm on the fence if this buyer might be problematic in the future.
title: Would you block this guy?

text:
Told to file report on buyer for fraudulent return,  I did, nothing happened

I sold a sapphire blue rhinestone necklace, buyer requested a return because the color did not match the dress she wanted to wear it with. While I am not responsible for how colors appear on her computer/phone screen, said ok, I will take the return BUT...buyer needs to pay return shipping - this is stated very clearly in my terms right under the descriptions, because I offer free shipping, buyers pay for returns, this has been my policy for 26 years on Ebay. Buyer shipped the package back - in her return details she stated item did not match the description or photos!! WHAT? I immediately called Ebay to complain - was told to file a report on her so I could appeal any defect resulting from that. I filed a report. Three days later, the package arrived. I called Ebay to ask them what I should do now. I was told to refund her, then appeal the defect. I did not want to refund her until this was resolved - I was told I had to refund her THEN appeal. I refunded her - defect is STILL THERE and today they have charged me $7.50 for return shipping. I just tried to appeal and was told CASE CLOSED by the automated agent who has NEVER been able to help me. So now I am out $15 (on shipping) because this person's monitor/phone screen showed her a color that was not correct. I am waiting for Ebay phone lines to open to speak with a human who can read the notes of the situation and hopefully help me. Any advice would be appreciated.
title: Told to file report on buyer for fraudulent return,  I did, nothing happened

text:
Open letter to the Head of Refunds Dispute Divisions & eBay CEO

Open letter to the Head of Refunds Dispute Divisions & eBay CEO Re Order: 03-14288-17535 eBay automatically issued a refund to one of my buyers and told the buyer they could keep the item. I NEVER SELECTED THIS OPTION when I set up my Seller Hub! The only thing that I set up was to allow a return of an item within 30 days. Unknown to me at the time I said I would allow returns within 30 days, eBay's Refund Preferences setting AUTOMATICALLY DEFAULTED to issuing a refund to a buyer for any issue concerning the item, as well as, an AUTOMATIC DEFAULT setting to tell the buyer they could keep the item. In a very recent discussion with an eBay Customer Service representative, the eBay representative said that these automatic settings were initiated because of something I, the seller, must have set. I ONLY said that refunds were allowed within 30 days BUT I NEVER SELECTED THE AUTOMATIC OPTION to return payment WITHOUT QUESTION. eBay told me, the seller had reported the item as damaged. eBay then told the buyer to keep the item - not to return it! In fact, when I reached out to the buyer, she told me that she never said the item or packaging was damaged. The buyer's only issue was that she found an infinitesimal spot on the collar of the sweater shell. eBay took that as "damage" and AUTOMATICALLY issued a refund and told the buyer to keep the item. The buyer NEVER even reached out to me - PERIOD! In my telephone conversation with the eBay Customer Service representative, I stated: "WHAT PERSON IN THEIR RIGHT MIND WOULD AGREE TO SUCH A REFUND PREFERENCE SETTING?! Why this SETTING even exists is UNBELIEVABLE! In fact, the eBay representative had to guide me through the myriad of eBay settings to turn the automatic settings OFF – SINCE I DID NOT KNOW WHERE TO LOCATE THESE SETTINGS IN THE FIRST PLACE!!! THIS IS AN EBAY SOFTWARE ISSUE NOT A SELLER SETTING ISSUE. I AM APPEALING THE FINAL DECISION BECAUSE EBAY NEVER SOUGHT MY INPUT IN THE FIRST PLACE BEFORE AUTOMATICALLY ISSUING A REFUND AND TELLING THE BUYER SHE COULD KEEP THE ITEM!!! I AM STILL APPEALING THE FINAL DECISION BECAUSE THIS WHOLE TRANSACTION WAS INAPPROPRIATE. Because the eBay's Refund Preference setting was automatically defaulted to refunding the buyer and telling the buyer she could keep the item, EBAY SHOULD REIMBURSE ME!!! It is not like eBay doesn't make a ton of money on a daily basis. PLEASE SEND THIS COMPLAINT UP THE CHAIN OF COMMAND TO SOMEONE WHO CAN ACTUALLY MAKE THE DECISION TO REIMBURSE ME FOR THIS TRANSACTION! Show some consideration PLEASE!
title: Open letter to the Head of Refunds Dispute Divisions & eBay CEO

Buyer is Requesting a Refund

However, when I priced the art at the restrike price of $300, he bought it. Now, he is requesting a refund saying that my description didn't match. I sent him a verbatim copy of the description used by the auction house who sold it to me. It's the same description that I used on Ebay. I knew it wasn't a vintage print because of the price. If it was vintage, it would have been priced at around $5,000. He thought he was getting a steal when he bought it at $300. Unfortunately, I didn't put "No Returns Accepted" in my terms. So Ebay took his word over mine and froze my account for the $300 the item sold for. Did I do anything wrong?
