text:
eBay Is Actively Promoting Unprotected Classified Ad Scams for High-Value Sales - my trust is gone

I've been a buyer and seller on eBay for 26 years, and I am raising something that I think deserves serious attention from the community and from eBay leadership: the eBay platform is currently surfacing, promoting, and in some cases actively sponsoring 'classified ad' listing scams for high-value Apple computers, with zero buyer protections in place. Here's a specific example. A listing for an Apple Mac Studio M2 Ultra (24-core CPU, 76-core GPU, 64GB, 1TB, with AppleCare+) has been sitting on the platform for several weeks at $1,950 — approximately $1,500 below what this machine sells for through legitimate channels. The listing uses urgency language ("Fast Sale! Grab It Before It's Gone!"), shows ebay-driven engagement metrics ("384 viewed in the last 24 hours," "277 people are watching"), and is formatted to look indistinguishable from a standard eBay auction. When I clicked "Make Offer", and entered a dollar mount eBay only then warned me that the transaction would take place entirely off eBay's systems and was not covered by eBay's purchase protection policy. Take a quick look at listings for Mac Studio and Mac Mini computers and you will see many other similar 'classified ads' with deals that are too good to be true. Let that sink in. eBay showed me the listing. eBay promoted it. eBay generated urgency around it. eBay sent notifications to my phone about it. And then eBay warned me that if I got scammed, I'd be on my own. It gets worse. The listing page states in fine print that "seller feedback scores exclude classified listings." Read that carefully: negative experiences from classified ad transactions are scrubbed from a seller's rating. In choosing to do this, eBay has gutted their feedback system to actively conceal the track record of exactly the accounts most likely to defraud buyers. This isn't an oversight — it's architecture that protects bad actors and keeps them operational, and it has destroyed my trust in their overall feedback system and the entire platform. This is not a gray area. Classified ad listings for $3,000+ computers priced at half of market value with seller feedback scores in the double digits, which are then routed off platform are not legitimate sales. They are a well-documented scam pattern. And the fact that eBay is algorithmically amplifying them is a fundamental breach of the trust that makes this marketplace function. The practical consequence for me is this: I'm in the market for a $3,000+ Mac Studio. I should be buying it on eBay. Instead, I'm going elsewhere — because I can no longer tell a legitimate seller from a scammer. That ambiguity has poisoned the entire category. My question to eBay is straightforward: why? eBay collects listing fees on classified ads regardless of outcome. I'd like to believe that's not the calculation driving these decisions. But the system as designed — promoted listings, visua...
title: eBay Is Actively Promoting Unprotected Classified Ad Scams for High-Value Sales - my trust is gone

text:
Scams! LEGO Star Wars: Imperial Shuttle (10212) x2

OK. I'm an idiot - but I hope this can help others while I wait for eBay to get on the ball and take care of these fraudulent transactions. One item, I won via an auction, [Removed by moderator] . The item location was listed as Frederick Michigan, United States. With Priority Mail shipping from USPS. However, exactly 3 days later I get shipping information that state its shipping via "Global Hauls", coming from Germany! Searching Global Hauls, it comes up as a scammers shipping site, that was just registered less than a year ago. The second item (same product, different sellers), I was given a 'second chance offer' for the item I didn't win via the auction . This item location was listed as Coal Run, Ohio, United States. With Priority Mail shipping from USPS. However, again, exactly 3 days later I get shipping information that state its shipping via "Logistics-GFX", also coming from Germany! Searing Logistics-GFX, It also comes up as a suspect site that was registered less than 6 months ago. Both sellers sent me a message with the same format and wording: Hi, your item has been shipped. Tracking number *********. GFX Logistics courier service. Tracking details can be viewed via the direct link. ********** Link provided Here********* T he parcel was sent as a gift. No additional fees for you! Have a nice day! The other was the exact same wording, except shipping and tracking was via Global Hauls. I'm thinking that others may have paid these guys via second chance offers and other fraudulent means via eBay. I learned a valuable lesson, always look at the users country of origin before you trust the item is real and is actually in the US. I hope eBay figures this out asap, as I'm out a considerable amount of money with these two purchases.
title: Scams! LEGO Star Wars: Imperial Shuttle (10212) x2

While Venmo is a wonderful and convenient Payment option for Buyers, recently it has been linked to numerous "Scamming" operations which unfortunately is on the rise. (use in the private Sector) *Is the seller protected By EBAY against these rising issues as Scammers are becoming more "advanced" in their abilities to find ways to "override" automated/automatic Payments with "delays", "false Numbers to clarify for issues", "accounts moved" are examples: ending with Money Being Lost by Private Sellers ( I worded this badly, I apologize) In my area, Banks are placing these accounts as RED FLAG Options and to be Used with caution. There are daily reports of People selling Privately, only to lose their Money. I have chosen EBAY as a 3rd Party Entity as a Protection against these Risks. Thank you so much for your thoughts on this. I love this Community Concept of discussion.
title: Venmo being added as payment Option

text:
The Supreme Court, Tariffs and ebay Sellers

Yesterday, the US Supreme Court invalidated a number of tariffs which had been created by Executive Orders under the authority of an Act which the Court decided did not, in fact, give the Executive branch the authority to create those tariffs. 1. The order does not invalidate all tariffs. Nor did the Court rule whether tariffs are bad or good. It simply invalidated certain specific tariffs because the Administration did not have authority to enact those tariffs, in part, because the Constitution gives primary tariff power to the US Congress, not the President. 2. There was some question whether the decision invalidated the Suspension of the de minimis $800 rule along with the tariffs. Whatever the answer to that question, the Administration issued a new executive order later in the day reaffirming the suspension, effective as of Feb 24. So, for all intents and purposes, the de minimis rule is still suspended. It is possible, maybe even likely, that this most recent order will be challenged in court because it relies on the same authorization the Court just said was invalid for enacting tariffs. But keep in mind that Congress had passed a law (The Big Beautiful Bill) which ends the de minimis rule as of July 1, 2027. The wheels of Justice grind slowly, as they say, and so unless a case is decided before July 1, 2027, or Congress amends the law, any decision will have little real impact. 3. There are other laws that grant the Executive some power over tariffs, and yesterday, after the court decision, at least one executive order was issued enacting a 10% flat tariff for 150 days, the maximum allowed by the law. Without going into detail, I'll just say it is likely we will see more tariffs enacted through some of these other methods, and also possibly through Congress. So tariffs are not going away. The decision is a speed bump on the tariff road, not the end of the road. 4. The decision has no direct impact on tariffs imposed on the US by other countries, it only applies to tariffs we imposed on other countries. While there may now be some changes made as the parties deal with this decision, it is safe to say that we, as ebay sellers, need to fill out the "Country of Origin" in the listing flow if we want to sell internationally. 5. Refunds. The court was basically silent on refunds of tariff payments already made under these now invalid tariffs, but litigation has already begun, with businesses trying to get refunds from the US Treasury for tariffs they had paid. However, the average consumer is not in a position to request a refund. You would need clear documentary evidence that you paid the tariff. If you bought an item at WalMart and WalMart passed the cost of the tariff onto you, you aren't eligible, because WalMart paid the actual tariff into the US Treasury, not you. All in all, the direct impact on most of us as ebay sellers will likely be small, especially if the de minimis suspension continues....
title: The Supreme Court, Tariffs and ebay Sellers
